setting:
A classic closed-circle manor mystery set in rural England in the late 1920s. On a stormy autumn evening, a select group of guests has gathered at an old country estate for a formal masquerade and private supper. By nightfall, the roads are flooded, the grounds are impassable, and the household is sealed off from the outside world until morning. The lead investigator must already be present inside the estate before the isolation begins, having been specifically invited by the eventual victim to witness or advise on a planned private revelation, accusation, or reckoning later that evening.

The story should feel like a traditional fair-play whodunit: atmospheric, elegant, and deduction-centered. It must be historically and technologically consistent with the late 1920s. No modern communications, digital devices, surveillance systems, database searches, or advanced forensic procedures may be used. The mystery must unfold through interviews, conflicting testimony, timing discrepancies, room access, physical traces, written documents, household objects, and careful reasoning. The investigator's presence must be narratively justified as part of the evening's social arrangement, not as someone who conveniently arrives from outside after the murder.

The estate should provide a rich but finite investigative space, including rooms such as a library, study, ballroom, conservatory, dining room, guest wing, servant corridors, and private chambers. The characters should be socially plausible for the period and setting: aristocratic family members, family friends, staff, professional associates, or dependents, each carrying secrets, anxieties, and conflicting interests. Themes may include inheritance, scandal, debt, blackmail, jealousy, class tension, and fear of public disgrace.

The crime should be designed as a layered but fair mystery with one true culprit, one victim, at least four serious suspects, at least eight evidence items, at least one red herring arc, at least two alibi checks, and at least one deliberate attempt to mislead the investigation. The hidden truth must support a long-form investigation with at least fifteen substantial plot beats, culminating in a final confrontation grounded in a clear and coherent chain of evidence.

victim.name: Sir Alistair Sterling
victim.description: A wealthy, stern industrialist in his late 60s, known for his vast estate and a recent, mysterious change in his social circle.

culprit.name: Arthur Sterling
culprit.description: Sir Alistair's only nephew and presumed heir. An elegant but desperate man with a penchant for high-stakes gambling.
culprit.means: Access to the estate's conservatory and knowledge of the heart medication Alistair already took, allowing for a lethal interaction.
culprit.motive: Alistair intended to sign a new will tonight disinheriting Arthur in favor of a newly discovered illegitimate daughter.
culprit.opportunity: He slipped away during the masquerade while everyone was in the ballroom, using the servant corridors to reach the study.
culprit.alibi: Claims he was in the smoking room alone, but no one can verify his presence for a crucial forty-minute window.

suspects.name: Arthur Sterling
suspects.description: Sir Alistair's only nephew and presumed heir. An elegant but desperate man with a penchant for high-stakes gambling.
suspects.means: Access to the estate's conservatory and knowledge of the heart medication Alistair already took, allowing for a lethal interaction.
suspects.motive: Alistair intended to sign a new will tonight disinheriting Arthur in favor of a newly discovered illegitimate daughter.
suspects.opportunity: He slipped away during the masquerade while everyone was in the ballroom, using the servant corridors to reach the study.
suspects.alibi: Claims he was in the smoking room alone, but no one can verify his presence for a crucial forty-minute window.

suspects.name: Eleanor Vance
suspects.description: Sir Alistair’s private secretary, recently hired. She is actually his illegitimate daughter, though only Alistair and the investigator knew this.
suspects.means: Constant access to Alistair's personal items and drinks.
suspects.motive: Resentment for years of neglect, though she truly desired a relationship rather than his money.
suspects.opportunity: Was seen near the study shortly before the body was discovered, delivering correspondence.
suspects.alibi: Claims to have been in the library preparing the new will documents, but the library was empty when the investigator checked.

suspects.name: Colonel Reginald Thorne
suspects.description: An old military friend of Sir Alistair with a distinguished career and a very thin wallet.
suspects.means: Carries a ceremonial dagger and has knowledge of poisons from his time in the colonies.
suspects.motive: Alistair was calling in a massive personal debt that would have bankrupted the Colonel and stripped him of his honors.
suspects.opportunity: Left the ballroom for 'fresh air' on the covered veranda, which has a window leading into the study.
suspects.alibi: States he was on the veranda, but his boots are suspiciously dry for someone out in a storm.

suspects.name: Martha Gable
suspects.description: The head housekeeper at Blackwood Manor for twenty years. Efficient, cold, and observant.
suspects.means: Full run of the house and knowledge of the household rhythms.
suspects.motive: She had been systematically stealing and selling family heirlooms; Alistair had discovered the missing items that morning.
suspects.opportunity: She was moving between the kitchens and the dining hall, passing the study multiple times.
suspects.alibi: Assisted by the kitchen staff, though they admit she was 'unaccounted for' during the heavy rain peak.

motive: Arthur discovered that Sir Alistair was rewriting his will to leave the entire Sterling fortune to Eleanor Vance. Facing immediate ruin from London debt collectors, Arthur needed to ensure his uncle died before the new will was signed at midnight.
method: Arthur extracted Digitalis (foxglove) concentrate from the conservatory and spiked Alistair's private decanter of brandy. The poison, combined with Alistair's existing heart condition, triggered a fatal cardiac arrest that appeared at first glance to be natural causes.

true_timeline.summary: Guests arrive at Blackwood Manor as the storm begins to intensify.
Sir Alistair privately reveals to Inspector Vane that he will sign the new will at midnight and expects a 'troublesome reaction'.
Arthur is seen arguing heatedly with Alistair near the conservatory; Alistair tells him he is 'finished'.
Arthur sneaks back into the conservatory to crush foxglove leaves into a concentrated vial.
The masquerade supper begins. All guests wear masks. Arthur uses a servant's passage to enter the study and spike the brandy.
Alistair complains of a headache and retires to his study to review the new will before the midnight signing.
Alistair drinks the poisoned brandy and collapses.
Arthur enters the study through the servant passage, places a bloody letter opener near the body to suggest a struggle, and steals the unsigned will.
Martha Gable discovers the body while bringing a late tea service. The house is alerted.
Inspector Vane declares the roads are flooded and the house is sealed. Investigation begins.

evidence_items.name: Crystal Brandy Decanter
evidence_items.description: The decanter is half-empty. A faint, bitter herbal residue is found at the bottom, inconsistent with high-quality brandy.

evidence_items.name: Bloody Letter Opener
evidence_items.description: A silver letter opener with a small amount of blood on the tip. It was found three feet from the victim.

evidence_items.name: Crushed Foxglove Petals
evidence_items.description: Several bruised petals of Digitalis Purpurea found caught in the floorboards near the study's servant entrance.

evidence_items.name: The Unsigned Will
evidence_items.description: A legal document naming 'Eleanor Vance, my natural daughter' as sole beneficiary. It is crumpled and was found in a wastepaper basket in the servant's hall.

evidence_items.name: Muddy Boots (Size 10)
evidence_items.description: Men's dress boots with distinctive yellow clay from the conservatory gardens. They match the size of Arthur Sterling.

evidence_items.name: Stolen Diamond Brooch
evidence_items.description: A piece of Sterling family jewelry reported missing months ago. It was hidden inside a flour sack.

evidence_items.name: Blue Silk Masquerade Mask
evidence_items.description: Arthur's mask, which has a small stain of what appears to be green plant sap on the interior lining.

evidence_items.name: Threatening Telegram
evidence_items.description: A telegram addressed to Colonel Thorne demanding payment by the 15th or 'consequences will follow'.

red_herrings.explanation: The bloody letter opener suggests a struggle or a stabbing attempt. However, Alistair has no puncture wounds. Arthur used Alistair's own letter opener to prick his own finger and smear the blade to frame Eleanor, who had used the opener earlier that day to open mail.
